source_text:
10. Reference Line

a. General. Use only those references that bear directly on the subject at hand. Avoid unnecessary or complicated references. Many letters may not need a reference, while others are complete with a reference to only the latest communication in a series. List references in the order they appear in the text. Always mention cited references in the text. Additionally, when citing a reference it is not necessary to include the subject of the reference. However, the subject may be included, following all other required elements, if it aids in clarifying or better identifying the reference.

b. Avoiding Most NOTAL References. A not-to-all (NOTAL) reference is a document that some addressees neither hold nor need. Avoid NOTAL references whenever possible. If a NOTAL reference is unavoidable, follow the reference in the reference line with the term "NOTAL" in parentheses. The following paragraphs discuss various referencing situations and an appropriate action for each situation:

(1) If it is known that the action addressee does not have a reference listed in your correspondence, include it as an enclosure to your correspondence or refer to it in the text. However, enclosing an existing, stand-alone document belonging to another Navy organization or another Service as an enclosure is now allowed in directives. If needed, include as a reference.

(2) If you would like to provide a copy of a reference listed in your correspondence to a "Via" or "Copy to" addressee, annotate "w/ref (x)" in parentheses to the right of the addressee you send it to (Substitute the appropriate reference letter for x). Only include this annotation on the copy for the addressee you are sending the reference to. Do not include the distribution of a reference to "Via" or "Copy to" addressees on the original letter.

c. Format. Type "Ref:" at the left margin on the second line below the subject line. Use a lowercase letter in parentheses before the description of every reference. If you have only one reference, list it as "Ref: (a)". Four spaces follow the colon. References are listed in alphabetical order, a through z. If you have more than 26 references, continue with (aa), (ab), etc. If the entry is longer than one line, line the second line under the first word after the heading.

d. Citing Various Types of References

(1) Naval correspondence requires (a) the SNDL originator short title, (b) the type of correspondence ("ltr" or "memo"), (c) the SSIC, (d) the originator's code by itself or in a serial number, and (e) the date. If the reference was not dated, type "(undated)" as illustrated below.

(2) A business letter requires (a) the company name, (b) the term "ltr", and (c) the date.

(3) An e-mail requires (a) the SNDL originator's short title, (b) the term "e-mail," (c) the type of correspondence ("ltr" or "memo"), (d) the SSIC, (e) the originator's code by itself or in a serial number, and (f) the date.

(4) A message requires (a) the originator's Plain Language Address as shown in the "From:" line of the message (if listed, do not include the office code) and (b) the complete datetime-group. When referencing general messages, include in parentheses the general message type (All Navy (ALNAV), Naval Administrative (NAVADMIN), All Marine Corps (ALMAR), etc.) and number/year.

(5) Endorsements cite references depending on whether you want to mention them in passing or highlight a particular one.

(6) A telephone conversation or meeting requires (a) "PHONCON" or "Mtg"; (b) the activity's SNDL short title, the office code, the individual's name, and (c) the date. Follow the information for the first individual with a forward slash and repeat the information for the second individual.

(7) A Navy instruction requires (a) the SNDL short title combined with the term "INST" and (b) the SSIC with the consecutive number and, if any, a revision letter. If referencing a large instruction, do not call out the chapter, section, or paragraph in the reference line, instead, identify them when using the reference in the text of the correspondence.

(8) A Navy notice requires (a) the SNDL short title of issuer combined with the term "NOTE" and the SSIC, (b) the serial number, (c) the date, and (d) the cancellation date enclosed in parenthesis (e.g., (Canc: Aug 08)). If referencing a large notice, do not call out the chapter, section, or paragraph in the reference line, instead, identify them when using the reference in the text of the correspondence.

(9) A DoD directive or instruction requires (a) the short title of issuer with either Instruction or Directive, (b) the SSIC with consecutive number, and (c) the date with the month spelled out. If referencing a large instruction or directive, do not call out the chapter, section, or paragraph in the reference line, instead, identify them when using the reference in the text of the correspondence.

(10) A DoD publication requires (a) the short title of issuer (b) the publication number, (c) the publication type, (d) the title, and (e) the date.

(11) A form requires (a) the issuer, (b) the form number, and (c) the issue or revision date.

(12) A report that has a Report Control Symbol requires (a) the report title, (b) the issuer, and (c) the report number.

(13) A Navy publication requires (a) the issuer and (b) the publication number.

(14) Code of Federal Regulations requires (a) the title number, (b) the term "CFR", (c) the part or chapter number, and (d) the section number (optional).

(15) Federal Register (FR) requires (a) the volume number, (b) the term "FR", and (c) the page number.

(16) A United States Code requires (a) the title number, (b) the term "U.S.C.", (c) the section symbol (§) [Alt+0167], and (d) the section number. Do not include spaces in the term "U.S.C."

(17) An Executive Order requires (a) the term "E.O." and (b) the order number.

(18) "My" and "Your" Optional. To cite an earlier communication between your activity and the action addressee, you may substitute a personal pronoun for the issuing activity. To prevent confusion, avoid "my" and "your" in the reference line of a letter that has more than one action addressee.
plain_language_rule: The Reference line is an optional header element used to cite correspondence or documents that bear directly on the subject. References must be listed in the order they appear in the text, and all cited references must be mentioned in the body text. The Ref: label is typed at the left margin on the second line below the subject line, followed by four spaces. Each reference is assigned a lowercase letter in parentheses, starting with (a) and continuing alphabetically through z; if more than 26 references exist, continue with (aa), (ab), etc. If a reference entry extends beyond one line, the continuation line aligns under the first word after the heading. NOTAL (not-to-all) references should be avoided when possible; if unavoidable, annotate with "NOTAL" in parentheses after the reference. When a reference is not held by all addressees, include it as an enclosure or reference it in the text. When providing a copy of a reference to specific Via or Copy to addressees, annotate "w/ref (x)" on the copy only, not on the original letter. Naval correspondence references require: originator short title, correspondence type (ltr/memo), SSIC, originator code or serial, and date. Undated references are marked "(undated)". Specific formats apply for business letters, e-mails, messages, endorsements, telephone conversations/meetings, Navy instructions and notices, DoD directives/instructions/publications, forms, reports, Navy publications, CFR, Federal Register, U.S.C., and Executive Orders. To cite earlier correspondence between the originator and action addressee, "My" and "Your" may be used as substitutes, but should be avoided when there is more than one action addressee.
renderer_impact: Header section Reference line rendering (optional); placement on second line below Subject line (one blank line gap); four-space offset after colon; left-margin alignment; continuation line alignment under first word after heading; lowercase lettering in parentheses (a), (b), (c)...(z), (aa), (ab), etc.; alphabetical ordering of references; NOTAL annotation support; w/ref (x) annotation for Via/Copy to distribution variants; reference entry formatting per document type (naval correspondence, business letters, e-mails, messages, endorsements, PHONCON/Mtg, instructions, notices, DoD directives, forms, reports, publications, CFR, FR, U.S.C., E.O.)
notes: CRITICAL PLACEMENT RULE: Reference line is on the SECOND line under the Subject line - this means one blank line between Subject and Ref. Four spaces follow the colon (vs. two spaces for From line, six spaces for To line, five spaces for Via line, three spaces for Subj line). Manual explicitly requires lowercase letters in parentheses for reference identifiers: (a), (b), (c), etc. through (z), then (aa), (ab), etc. if more than 26 references. References must be listed in alphabetical order by assigned letter, but the letter assignment follows the order references appear in the text. CONTINUATION LINE ALIGNMENT: If a reference entry is longer than one line, the second line starts under the first word after the heading (not under the Ref: label). RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER SECTIONS: Reference line is an optional header element used when citing prior correspondence or documents relevant to the subject. Manual states 'Always mention cited references in the text' - this is a content requirement, not just formatting. Section 7-2.11 (C7-012) covers Enclosure line; manual explicitly states 'Never list an item in both the enclosure line and reference line of the same letter.' NOTAL references require special handling - avoid when possible, annotate with (NOTAL) when unavoidable. When providing reference copies to specific addressees, annotate on copies only, not on original. ENFORCEABILITY marked 'partial' because: (1) reference content accuracy depends on external document verification; (2) alphabetical ordering and letter assignment are deterministic but reference text formatting varies by document type; (3) 'mention cited references in the text' requires body text analysis. AMBIGUITY: Manual states references are 'listed in alphabetical order, a through z' but also says 'List references in the order they appear in the text' - these appear to conflict. Interpretation: assign letters (a), (b), (c) in order of appearance in text, then list them in that order (which is both order-of-appearance AND alphabetical by assigned letter). DO NOT activate until renderer geometry confirms Ref line placement at Subject_baseline + 2 leading units (one blank line gap).
